data.title: Centene's Earnings Reset Opens The Door To A Re-Rating (Upgrade)
data.description:
Centene Corporation’s (CNC) investment setup looks totally different now. Things got shaken up by a crisis, but there’s also a real shot at earnings bouncing back for the next few years. Since I last called it a Hold, the stock has fallen from $41.18 to $33.20, a 19.4% drop. That drop already reflects most of the obvious damage and all the Medicaid redetermination mess.

People were right to worry, considering the significant earnings pressure and margin compression reflected in the 2025 results, including adjusted diluted EPS of $2.08. Still, focusing just on those headlines misses what’s changed: management is now guiding to a recovery in margins and earnings and a believable way back to stable, normal EPS power, something both the Board and management are now willing to put in official guidance.

Medicaid Base

The main reason to be optimistic is how managed Medicaid holds up, plus the re-pricing built into Centene’s 2026 plan. For 2026, Medicaid is expected to generate roughly $88 billion in premium and service revenue, remaining the company’s largest segment. The 2025 GAAP numbers looked awful: cost trends remained elevated, and the consolidated HBR shot up from 88.3% in 2024 to 91.9%.

But if you look closer, Medicaid profitability actually got better in the second half of 2025. Management said the “fourth quarter health benefits ratio of 93.0% was consistent with expectations,” and it’s behavioral health, not big acute care cost spikes, causing most of the trouble. The 2026 forecast depends on Medicaid rates going up by a mid-4% range, even though they expect member months to drop 5% to 6%, which should still mean revenue above $88 billion, even as state redeterminations keep causing members to drop off.

Medicaid margins are stabilizing and improving, driven by tighter clinical programs and network optimization. That’s central to restoring profitability, and management expects continued margin stability with potential upside as these cost actions take hold.

Medicare Mix Shift

Medicare is a key area where results could improve. The PDP side should drive about $7.5 billion in premium growth, bringing total PDP revenue to roughly $34.5 billion. By the end of the year, the revenue mix is expected to be 41% Medicare Advantage and 59% PDP, reflecting a shift toward PDP growth driven by pricing and enrollment dynamics. That shift comes from changes in benefit design and subsidy dynamics tied to the IRA. Those changes mean the direct subsidy is going up from $143 to $200, reflecting higher pharmacy cost trends as outlined by management.

Medicare Advantage membership will decline slightly by design, as the company focuses on higher-yield members rather than volume. For Medicare PDP, they are targeting a roughly 2% pretax margin, down from “a good year in the 3s.” A higher HBR is largely driven by higher premiums and pharmacy costs under the new structure, keeping overall segment economics stable.

Marketplace remains a key area of uncertainty. The expiration of enhanced APTCs is expected to reduce Marketplace premium and service revenue by around $8 billion in 2026. That creates a meaningful revenue headwind, especially alongside declining Medicaid membership. For 2026, total premium and service revenue is guided to $170 billion to $174 billion. Management has explicitly incorporated the APTC expiration into pricing and guidance assumptions.

Cost Structure

Operational leverage could be better than folks think. The SG&A ratio is down to 7.4% for 2025, and they’re keeping it steady in 2026. That’s coming from tight expense controls and the fact that Medicare PDP and core Medicaid (which have lower SG&A) are making up a bigger part of the mix, while Marketplace is shrinking. Capex is set at $800 million. Net investment and other income should come in at $1.4 billion. Interest expense is expected to be between $620 million and $650 million.

They’re still focused on paying down debt, aiming to finish 2025 with a 46.5% debt-to-capital ratio. Investment income will drop compared to 2024, but that's because they’re assuming lower Fed funds rates, not because anything’s wrong with how the managed care business is running.

Earnings Reset

Earnings power is where things really reset. The 2025 adjusted diluted EPS dropped hard, $2.08, way down from $7.17 in 2024. For 2026, though, they’re saying it should get back above $3.00, which would be over 40% up from this lower point. If you look further out, the company’s steady-state or “through-cycle” EPS for 2027 and after seems set to land meaningfully above current depressed levels, with analyst consensus estimates around $4 per share for 2027.

At the current stock price, $32.20, the market is giving this approximately an 11 multiple based on consensus EPS near $3, which is a lot lower than what you usually see for this sector or for their peers. The market isn’t really pricing in much credit for management starting to fix the cost and margin side of things.

Key Risks

Plenty could still go wrong. Higher interest rates, new rules, or drops in membership could swing things around. The biggest risk is still some new policy change, especially around Medicaid rates or how states pay for things. If something does go off track, it’s probably going to come from a new big-picture shock or losing more Medicaid contracts. Right now, nothing like that shows up in their current business or what they’ve told investors to expect. They could also miss out on getting margins back up, especially if medical or pharmacy costs climb faster than they can push prices or cut expenses. In that case, earnings could fall back below $3 a share.

Still, after the most recent update, it looks like they’ve taken the hit already. Current guidance incorporates updated assumptions around rates, costs, and margins consistent with recent performance trends.

Valuation Gap

The stock looks cheap compared to how things usually get valued. The market just isn’t giving normal credit to a company that’s no longer facing an all-or-nothing risk. They’re guiding for a strong year in 2026, with better margins, tighter spending, and more premium business. Because the market hammered the stock for the 2025-2026 slump, there’s now a real case for a bounce. The setup is there for earnings to climb back to levels above current consensus expectations if margin recovery exceeds guidance, with more upside possible if they squeeze out better margins, and a lot of the downside already seemed baked in after the asset write-downs and price drop.

People are still pretty negative on the stock, but that’s all based on stuff that’s already happened. Headlines keep focusing on write-downs and interest rate worries. The stock’s been all over the place, down 45% from the highs over the past year, including a wild 22% drop in one month before things calmed down. Most commentary just reacts to the latest news instead of actually looking ahead at what the company might earn if things stabilize.

The stock hasn’t gotten any kind of bump for possible improvement, just more pressure on the valuation. Basically, the market isn’t giving any credit for things getting better and sticking.

Execution Test

Looking ahead to 2026, that’s the main reference point. Management kept full-year guidance the same and ruled out buying back stock, so everything’s about getting margins and cash flow back up through the core business. The next year is make-or-break: they need to actually show margins improving in Medicaid and the Marketplace, hit the expected SG&A savings, avoid any new big losses or drops in membership, and get better results from the Medicare PDP segment now that the IRA law is in place. If any of those slip, especially if margins don’t recover or unexpected losses emerge, the whole argument has to be reconsidered. But if margins do come back and memberships stop sliding, there’s a lot more room for the stock to run.

Rating Upgrade

The stock should move up to a Buy rating. The risk and return look good now that the policy changes and impairment hits are mostly behind it, even if the market hasn’t adjusted yet. I’m not going to call it a Strong Buy, since there’s still some uncertainty about healthcare policy, and Medicaid could still surprise, though I think most of that risk is already reflected in the price. If they show a couple more quarters of better margins and stable funding, then maybe it makes sense to get more aggressive. For now, the evidence points to a clear case for being bullish on returns from here, with the business getting back to normal and margins improving likely to push the stock higher over the next few quarters.

data.title: Aeva Technologies: FMCW Single Seller Collides With A Pre-SOP Liquidity Chasm
data.description:
I rate Aeva Technologies, Inc. (AEVA) a Hold. Aeva has a technological single-seller status in Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave [FMCW] silicon photonics that are validated by Nvidia (NVDA), Daimler (DTGHF, DTRUY), and Top-Tier OEMs. The main point I consider is that the shift from legacy Time-of-Flight [ToF] LiDAR to FMCW for L3 autonomy is a structural inevitability. However, the temporal mismatch between Aeva's ~24-month liquidity runway and its 2028 mass-production timeline guarantees big stock dilution. More so, AEVA stock is trading at 28.64x forward EV/Sales that leaves almost no margin of safety for macroeconomic friction/automotive SOP delays.

The technology is a Strong Buy, but the pre-dilution capital structure is a Sell. This backs the formation of a balanced Hold rating until capital is raised.

The FMCW Architectural Shift

My Aeva stock’s long-term bullish thesis is based on a long-term industry shift from legacy ToF LiDAR to FMCW silicon photonics. This shift is beyond standard automotive hardware cycles. I have multiple points to answer. Why? The first factor is the Nvidia DRIVE Hyperion Integration Multiplier, as Aeva is now the reference 4D LiDAR for the Hyperion platform. This builds a design-in-default for OEMs utilizing Nvidia’s full-stack AV processing. As Hyperion is the foundational architecture for industry giants, Aeva bypasses fragmented multi-year OEM sensor evaluations. Moreso, as Nvidia integrates Aeva’s 4D velocity data into its core simulation and validation environments, OEM software stacks become dependent (inseparably) on deterministic instant velocity measurements. This development forms a strong software lock-in for a hardware sensor.

Now, the second factor is the Defense Sector Advantage. For more clarity, Aeva’s Forterra win exposes an important blind spot in legacy ToF sensors. The blind spot is that ToF lasers are highly visible to night-vision goggles [NVGs], and this renders them tactical liabilities. Aeva’s FMCW continuous wave is invisible to NVGs. So what? FMCW’s instant velocity detection and proprietary motion estimation back precise 6DoF [Degrees of Freedom] odometry in GPS-denied environments. The point is, Defense agencies need immediate perception in unstructured domains, and Aeva provides a stealth GPS-independent solution. With the defense market accelerating AI-edge adoption, this segment offers high-margin, short-cycle revenue to bridge the gap before automotive mass production.

On top of that, the third factor is the "Top 10" and "Top 5" OEM Tipping Point; a top European OEM is ripping out existing ToF infrastructure to shift to Aeva’s FMCW for its 2028 SOP standardized L3 platform. At the same time, a Top 5 OEM is developing a common vehicle platform utilizing Atlas Ultra across multiple car brands. This makes it clear that hitting highway-speed Level 3 autonomy at scale needs the zero-latency velocity data that only FMCW provides. All of the above, the LG Innotek Manufacturing De-Risking is the 4th factor of my thesis. By securing up to $50 million from LG Innotek (a supplier scaled for Apple), Aeva shifts heavy manufacturing CapEx off its balance sheet. LG Innotek’s involvement in launching the Omni 360-degree sensor opens up the physical AI and humanoid robotics TAM and is shifting Aeva from an automotive Tier-1 into a ubiquitous/predominant edge-perception silicon provider.

The 2026-2028 Liquidity Valley of Death

Ok, so now I expand on the negatives. Despite a technological moat, Aeva holds existential and valuation risks over the long term. First and foremost is the 2026-2028 Pre-SOP Liquidity Cavern. To expand here, Aeva’s financials present a temporal mismatch. Aeva has a gross cash use (operating cash flow less CapEx) of $119.7 million in 2025. Total available liquidity sits at $246.9 million ($121.9 million in cash/marketables + $125 million undrawn facility). Simple math points ~24 months of runway. However, the mass commercialization trigger [the Top 10 European OEMs’ Start of Production (SOP)] is slated for 2028. To be more simple, my point is Aeva must cross a 3-year desert with a 2-year canteen. In my opinion, a highly dilutive equity raise/the issuance of convertible debt is a certainty before the 2028 super-cycle begins. Current AEVA stockholders face high structural dilution that can suppress per-share value permanently.

Apart from that, there is an extreme valuation asymmetry. At ~$15 per share, Aeva trades at a high 52.05x EV/Sales TTM and 28.64x EV/Sales forward (based on $32.86 million 2026 consensus). This valuation prices in very, very smooth business execution of the $80 billion TAM base. In the automotive sector, SOP delays of 12 to 24 months are the historical norm, not a big exception. Any macro-led delay in the 2028 SOP timeline can compress this extreme multiple profile faster. To answer how? Wall Street can penalize AEVA stock for the prolonged cash burn, in my opinion.

Alongside this, there is the Reference vs. Requisite Illusion. Being the reference sensor on Nvidia’s Hyperion platform is a powerful hunting license, but it is not a binding purchase order. In that context, the automotive industry is very ruthless regarding Bill of Materials [BOM] costs. If a competitor makes a good enough software-defined velocity estimation using cheaper solid-state ToF sensors fused with high-res radar, then what? OEMs can swap out Aeva’s hardware to preserve margins.

Above all, Wall Street analysts can layer these with Physical AI working capital traps that can damage the mCap and AEVA stock price. More on this: Aeva targets a 5x increase in industrial sensor shipments (Omni) to accelerate non-auto revenue. Scaling hardware to this magnitude needs high working capital expansion. If the emerging humanoid robotics market scales slower than Aeva’s management thought it to be, Aeva will be burdened with carrying costs for its 100,000-unit automated final assembly lines. This can scale up the already risky cash burn rate.

Aeva Technologies Stock Long-Term Valuation Analysis

Aeva's current Enterprise Value [EV] is $869 million. I am using a forward EV/Sales method discounted back to PV. My base projections are based on the 2028 mass-commercialization consensus of $219.04 million (+186.47% Y/Y). I am assuming Aeva maintains its technological single-seller status, and a mature automotive/silicon hybrid EV/Sales multiple of 4.5x (near ⅓ of the current mid-term average) is justified for 2028 amid the ongoing correction. This is yielding a forward EV of ~$985 million. However, given the $119.7 million annual cash burn and $246.9 million total liquidity, Aeva will face a $120 million–$150 million capital shortfall before 2028. Raising this capital at current valuations means issuing roughly 10 million-12 million new shares. This can dilute the current 60.08 million share base by ~20%. I am applying a 20% discount rate to account for automotive SOP delay risks and adjusting for the inevitable 20% share dilution, and as a result, the fair value per share compresses. The derived long-term target price is $9.5-$11. At $15.67 (at the time of writing), the stock trades at a premium as the pre-SOP capital requirements are not priced in as of now.

What to Do With Aeva Technologies' Next Earnings?

Bulls should (in my opinion) scrutinize the Q1 2026 (expected early in May) gross cash use against the $119.7 million FY2025 baseline. Any quarterly cash burn over $30 million pulls the dilution event forward and can trigger a selloff. Secondly, the important factor is the demand from the Top 5 OEM joint development program, converting into a binding capitalized production award. Lastly, another important point to monitor is the early Omni shipments along with LG Innotek, as considerable physical AI revenue is the non-dilutive factor available to offset automotive NRE [Non-Recurring Engineering] lumpiness and extend the cash runway.

Conclusion

Overall, Aeva is a fundamentally superior technology company, but it is trapped in a pre-revenue temporal gap. I end this analysis by marking a Hold rating. Bulls should wait for the inevitable capital raise that will reset the valuation. Along with that, it can provide a de-risked entry point prior to the 2028 SOP super-cycle, in my opinion. To check the actionability of my Hold AEVA stock thesis, I will monitor the timing, size, and structure of the 2026/2027 capital raise. Apart from that, I will track the Top 5 OEM development contract conversions to Series Production. Finally, I will keep a check on revenue scaling in Defense (Forterra) to bridge the pre-2028 cash flow void.

data.title: Plains All American: Backwardation Might Ruin The Party
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In the past few years, Plains All American Pipeline, L.P. Common Units (PAA)(PAGP) told investors that it decreased its reliance on arbitrage and trade-based services to focus on the more stable, contracted, fee-based earnings. In practical terms, this meant emphasizing pipeline transport services, particularly those on long-term contracts and committed volumes, and de-emphasizing revenue from less stable ventures, such as time and location arbitrage.

The geopolitical tensions in the Middle East continue to disrupt the energy markets. Oil prices have reached multi-year highs. Equally important, the market is in backwardation, meaning that spot prices are higher than future prices. This is the litmus test of PAA claims.

The last time PAA reported the performance of the Transport & Logistics segment—the division that exploited oil & gas arbitrage and trading opportunities—was in Q3'21 (p.72). In some periods, the segment represented a significant portion of the company's consolidated earnings. Today, PAA no longer reports this segment, leaving us with only a directional, descriptive gauge of their profit contribution. Today, the oil market disruptions will put management's claims that PAA is predominantly a fee-for-service business to the test.

PAA's stock is up ~10% since the start of the hostilities in the Middle East. This reflects how the market is now pricing competing dynamics. The backwardated oil futures market impacts arbitrage and trading revenue. On the other hand, a rise in oil prices could be beneficial for the transport segment, as producers pump more volumes to capitalize on the high prices.

If the revenue tailwinds from market-priced contracts are more than offset by the trading and arbitrage revenue, this might create a disappointing Q1'26 and vice versa.

In a past article, I noted three potential catalysts going into 2026:

  1. A potential rebound in oil prices.
  2. The return of non-fee-based earnings opportunities, e.g. basis differentials.
  3. Close of the Canadian NGL asset sale and subsequent deleveraging.

Back then, I viewed these as separate earnings catalysts. The current market conditions raise a different question: will headwinds of a backwardated market outweigh the tailwinds of rising oil prices? My view is that this is not a base-case scenario but a key risk to consider as the market prices the benefits of the rising oil prices. The potential market reaction to Q1'26 earnings will depend on how much optimism has been baked into the price versus actual results. Again, the key problem is that PAA no longer discloses Supply & Logistics as a separate business line, limiting investor visibility.

I remain constructive on the stock given its low valuation and stable business model beyond periodic volatility. PAA's commitment to stable dividends offsets short-term disruptions, further supporting my bullish view.

Why Backwardation Matters?

As of December 2025, PAA had 76 million barrels of crude oil storage capacity across multiple sites in the US and Canada.

PAA says that a large portion of its storage capacity is contracted under long-term agreements, with revenue recognized ratably regardless of actual usage (p.F-18). They basically charge for making the capacity available when needed. This is a stable source of revenue that reflects the "tollbooth" business model that attracts many investors to this ticker. The table below shows the Remaining Performance Obligation for Terminalling, Storage, and Other services under PAA's long-term agreements:

Another type of contract tied to PAA's crude storage facilities is merchant services. In normal market conditions, spot oil prices are less than future oil prices. The difference in price reflects the cost of storing the barrels and the interest rate one would earn if they bought a risk-free income security instead of crude oil. Here's what traders, including PAA's own merchant division, do. They buy crude oil from oil & gas companies at the spot prices, find a cheap storage facility to store this crude, and simultaneously engage in a futures sales contract to sell that oil in the future, profiting from the price difference between the spot price and the futures price. In the past, PAA made a lot of money doing this, among other trading operations, such as price differentials (capitalizing on price differences between different locations).

The Shape Of The Futures Market Driven By Geopolitics

The current hostilities in the Middle East have disrupted the cash-and-carry arbitrage. Spot WTI prices are $94/barrel at the time of this writing, while Brent, the benchmark for world trade, settles at $108/barrel. The increase reflects the disruptions caused by the war, which impacts oil transport through the Strait of Hormuz.

The futures market reflects another key dimension to the story. The future prices (shown below) capture the sentiment that the hostilities won't last forever. Thus, today's prices might mirror disruptions that the market isn't pricing as given in the second half of 2026 and beyond.

How Higher Prices Could Help

Most of PAA's crude oil transport earnings are fee-based, applied to volumes transported. In their February 2026 update, the company said that fee-for-service would represent 85% of FY'26 adjusted EBITDA. This creates earnings visibility that underpins the bullish rating despite short-term disruptions.

Also, the current geopolitical environment could, in fact, be beneficial for PAA. Unlike producers in the Middle East, whose physical crude oil production has been disrupted by the hostilities, American producers, the main customers of PAA, don't suffer from such disruptions. US oil companies are reaping the rewards without the risk, benefiting from rising oil prices without the logistics disruptions. US oil & gas producers seem to be participating in filling the supply gap created by falling production in the Middle East. This means more business for PAA, as US producers open the valves. The Energy Information Agency increased its estimates for US oil production for 2026 and 2027.

U.S. crude oil production. Higher oil prices lead to more U.S. crude oil production in our forecast. We expect U.S. crude oil production will average 13.6 million barrels per day (b/d) in 2026 and rise to 13.8 million b/d in 2027. Our 2027 forecast is 0.5 million b/d higher than last month’s forecast. EIA Short-Term Outlook

Outlook, Valuation, And Risk

On February 25, 2026, PAA released an investor update. The report gives an FY'26 EBITDA guidance of 2.75 billion (+/- 75 million). However, the report was released three days before the war started on February 28, 2026 - the day of the first US strikes on Iran. The report assumes a WTI price of $60/barrel in the first half, rising to $65/barrel in the second half of the year (p.23). These assumptions clearly don't hold as of today. Based on the CME futures data, oil prices will average $81/barrel in the second half. Today's WTI spot price is $94, and the May deliveries are at ~$90/barrel.

In a small footnote in the investor's update, PAA states that for each $10/barrel increase in crude oil price, investors should expect a $40 million increase in adjusted EBITDA (p.11). However, this doesn't necessarily incorporate the geopolitical disruptions that are shaping the futures market today.

Assuming an average price of $85 in 2026, we'd assume a $90 million increase in adjusted EBITDA, above the company's $2.75 billion estimates (at midpoint). Applying this adjustment yields a forecasted $2.84 billion.

Based on PAA's share price of $22.40/share and a market cap of $15.6 billion, this updated adjusted EBITDA figure of $2.84 billion translates to an EV/EBITDA multiple of just 11.5x. Moreover, Seeking Alpha Quant puts P/Forward Cash Flows at 5.8x, a compelling valuation that underpins the bullish outlook in this piece.

Final Thoughts

PAA is a leading crude oil pipeline midstream company. In recent years, it has undergone a significant transformation, decreasing its reliance on volatile merchant and arbitrage trading in the oil & gas markets. As of the latest presentations, the company derives roughly 85% of its EBITDA from fee-for-service offerings. This stability, combined with its low valuation, underpins the buy rating.

The recent geopolitical tensions could encourage US oil & gas producers to move more oil to fill the gap left by the US/Iran War and the associated oil supply disruptions. PAA, in fact, provides a sensitivity table that shows that with each $10 increase in oil prices, investors should expect a $40 million increase in EBITDA. This seems priced by the market, as PAA shares rose by ~10% since the start of the hostilities roughly a month ago.

What this article highlights is what might not be priced in the market: the loss of the arbitrage and merchant activities because of a backwardated market.

data.title: Gladstone Capital: Is The 17% Discount Justified?
data.description:
BDCs currently occupy a unique niche in the investment community—they provide retail investors with access to private credit markets while distributing the majority of their taxable income as dividends. However, not all companies in the sector are created equal: different portfolios, credit quality, leverage, and risk-premium management can result in different BDCs.

In the following, we will examine Gladstone Capital (GLAD) in the BDC sector, with a special focus on the benchmark Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC).

BDC Premium/Discounts

Below is shown a chart with the current pricing of all BDCs:

At the time of writing, GLAD is traded at a 17% discount, while the NAV per share of the common stock declined from $21.34 to $21.13 for the last quarter. The benchmark ARCC is at close to a 10.43% discount, and the average BDC is around a 25.4% discount. The discount itself is not an indicator of undervaluation - it often reflects real risk or expectations of deterioration, though in GLAD's case, the metrics suggest the discount may be excessive relative to fundamentals

Market Capitalization

In the next chart are shown the market capitalizations. We see that GLAD is not among the biggest companies with its close to $407 million market capitalization, and we can't compare it with the biggest, ARCC, and its $13.24 billion market capitalization. The average for the sector is around $1.3 billion.

Portfolio Structure and Debt Percentage Per Company

ARCC and the average BDC are very similar in this comparison, with portfolio debt of approximately 81% and 84%, respectively, whereas GLAD's debt is approximately 95%.

3-Year NAV Total Return

The next chart shows the 3-year NAV total return:

For this metric, GLAD and the average BDC are very close. The 3-year return on NAV of GLAD is close to 29%, and the return of the average is around 35%. ARCC's 3-year return on NAV is around 16%. GLAD is close to the average, but its results were achieved with higher volatility and risk

3-Year Price Total Return

In this ratio, ARCC beats again, but GLAD is very close to it, even though it is currently trading among its lowest points.

3Y (NAV-Price return)

This chart shows the spread between price and net asset value to visualize the most undervalued stocks relative to their NAV performance. The ARCC is close to the average, while GLAD is among the better buys. It is important to clarify that a large spread is not to be underestimated; it can mean expected risk.

Non-Leverage Expenses/Total Assets

The two companies are very close to the average BDC if we are talking about costs of doing business, but GLAD is the lowest here.

3-Year Discount-Adjusted NAV Return

When we look at the historical NAV return and adjust it for the discount/premiums of a particular BDC, we see that GLAD is among the best expected performers, even more than the benchmark. This shows potential for higher returns, but the result is highly dependent on the discount and historical data.

Discount-Adjusted Expected ROE

We calculate the expected ROE using the historical loss of total investment income for each company over the last three years, and here GLAD is among the leaders again, while ARCC is close to the average BDC:

Bond Yields 5-Year Indicative

If we talk about credibility, GLAD is close to the average BDC, while ARCC is the best in this metric. ARCC carries a higher credit quality, and GLAD higher risk.

Discount-Adjusted ROE Spread to Leveraged Bonds

This metric shows the expected spread between equity and bonds for any particular company in the research. If we compare the historical returns of ARCC and GLAD to the expected return on their bonds, we see that with both, we can expect positive alternative yields. Both companies offer a positive spread, meaning that equity offsets the risk relative to debt.

Total Investment Income/Assets

This ratio is an indicator of the initial credit risk of a company's portfolio. ARCC and GLAD have quite similar asset yields, close to 10% for both, lower than the average BDC, around 11%.

An interesting point is that despite its closeness to the benchmark, GLAD does not rely on a high PIK income (only ~3.5% compared to ~8.5% average for the sector). This suggests that the positive effect arises primarily from realized and unrealized profits rather than from accumulated non-cash income. On the one hand, this is a better quality signal, but on the other hand, it indicates greater volatility.

Debt Yield as Reported by the Companies

ARCC is among the lowest here, while GLAD is close to the average.

Average Loss of Total Investment Income Over the Last 3 Years

ARCC is performing significantly better than the sector average, with a minimum loss of around -3% cоmpared tо an average of -11.66%. GLAD, on the other hand, stands out with a strоng pоsitive deviation оf 21.84%, but this probably comes with higher volatility. It is interesting here that despite the positive valuе, GLAD does not rely on high PIK income, as is the case with most of its peers' positive values.

Assets Yield After Loss Estimate 3 Years

ARCC is slightly below the average here with 9.42% vs. 9.52% for the Average BDC, suggesting a more conservative but stable return profile after losses. GLAD stands out again with a significantly higher asset yield of close to 12.32%

Standard Deviations

To summarize the data, we constructed a “sigma chart” that shows how much individual companies deviate from the sector average. On average, the sector shows moderate returns of close to 18-25%, but the standard deviation for price return is close to 28%. ARCC exhibits a strong positive bias in expected returns and operating efficiency, while GLAD deviates even mоre extreme, bоth pоsitively and negatively, оn various metrics, suggesting higher risk and greater volatility.

GLAD vs. BDC sector

  • Credit Quality

In the picture below, you can see all credit metrics of the BDC sector at the time of writing, using Moody's methodology:

The leverage ratio of the GLAD is close to 47%, which makes its asset coverage ratio cushion 41% - one of the biggest in the sector. The 3-year return on assets of the company is around 6.8%, while the average BDC has a close to 3.5% average calculated return. The percentage of the senior secured assets is around 86.4%, higher than the average BDC's 75%. The company has only close to 3.5% secured debt, and the problem loans are around 1.7%, which is close to the average BDC (1.55%). We see that GLAD's credit quality, based on the metrics, is very close to that of the benchmarks

  • Bond Yield Curve

The average asset yield of GLAD is around 9.8%, the average loss of yield is close to 2.5%, and for the last 3 years, the average loss/total income is around 22%-23%, as noted previously, which is one of the highest in the sector. The expected ROE for the common stock is around 12.9%, but because it is at a nearly 17% discount, the discount-adjusted ROE increases to 15.54%. These values ​​are derived from historical data over the past 3 years. The average BDC OTC bond has a close to 5.8% average yield to maturity. To make the proper comparison with the average BDC bond, we add leverage (from IBKR) to the OTC bond and consider a hypothetical capital structure with 200 total assets and 100 net assets. Under these conditions, the expected leveraged-bond ROE is close to 8.10%, which is close to 7% lower than GLAD. A spread of 7% is too significant to ignore, and for investors with a higher risk appetite, this difference may justify the additional risk. The comparison is illustrated below:

Conclusion

GLAD is trading at a significant discount of 17% and has shown solid performance relative to the BDC sector in both credit quality and return on equity. Historical yields, quality of earnings, and adjusted ROE suggest potential for high returns, with higher risk than the benchmark ARCC, but GLAD appears undervalued relative to its peers and highly profitable due to quality earnings, low PIK, and historically strong performance, resulting in a positive deviation from the BDC sector average profit/loss metric.

data.title: 5 Lessons That Completely Reshaped How I Approach Dividend Investing
data.description:
On the surface, dividend investing looks very simple: simply buy a diversified portfolio of stocks that pay attractive dividends and then sit back, relax, and let the dividends flow. However, the reality is that dividend investing is not that simple, as there are many hidden landmines that can quickly destroy your passive income stream and your wealth if you are not careful.

In this article, I am going to detail five lessons that completely reshaped how I approach dividend investing. Today, with dividend stocks staging a comeback, as evidenced by the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF's (SCHD) significant outperformance year to date relative to tech stocks (XLK) like Microsoft (MSFT) and Palantir (PLTR), and valuations remaining frothy for the broader S&P 500 (SPY) while remaining attractive for many high-yielding dividend stocks across the MLP (AMLP), REIT (VNQ), BDC (BIZD), industrial (XLI), consumer (XLP), and asset manager (BX) sectors, I believe a new golden age of dividend investing may very well be upon us. Thus, I think that these lessons are more important than ever.

Lesson 1: High Yield Is A Warning Label, Not An Objective

Probably the biggest lesson that dividend investors should keep in mind is that a high yield is a warning label rather than simply a buy signal. Sure, the higher the yield, the better, all else being equal, and, to be clear, my portfolio typically yields between 7% and 8%. However, whenever I see a stock yielding in the high single digits or low double digits, I immediately view that as presenting both opportunity and a major warning sign, unless the business model is typically priced at a very high yield due to low growth prospects.

Thus, I do find occasional mispriced opportunities like these to seize upon, and I'll use extra selectivity when I come across these, because generally, in the market, a yield that high means that they are signaling concern about the dividend and/or very weak growth prospects. Dividend cuts are one of the worst things that could possibly happen to a dividend investor, because not only does your income decline, but generally, the market value of your position does as well. Therefore, whenever you see a stock yielding in the high single digits to low double digits, you should immediately ask yourself, "Why is the market pricing this yield so high?" If everything seems wonderful with the stock, what am I missing?

Lesson 2: Payout Ratio - Measure It Right Or Pay The Price

The next important lesson that I learned is that the payout ratio is extremely important, and how you determine it is also just as important. For example, a company may have what appears to be a fully covered dividend based on a traditional earnings metric. For example:

  • For a REIT, its AFFO payout ratio may be well below 100.

  • For a midstream company, it may be its distributable cash flow payout ratio.

  • For a renewable power company, it may be its cash available for distribution.

  • Even for a traditional company, it might be earnings per share.

However, free cash flow is often important because if they have a significant capex surge due to investing in growth or due to an industry transition where they're trying to stave off competition, or even worse yet, they have a heavily leveraged balance sheet that commands a lot more of their capital, the dividend may very well end up being at risk. Several recent examples of this include the likes of W.P. Carey (WPC), Lumen Technologies (LUMN), and AT&T (T), all of which appeared to be covering their dividends just fine on a payout ratio basis using traditional metrics. However, if you looked under the hood, you would have seen that the real payout ratio was on much riskier footing, leading to all three of them cutting their dividends.

Lesson 3: Business Durability Matters More Than Current Yield

A third lesson is that business durability is far more important than the current yield. For example, you can often find high-yielding cyclical stocks that either pay out a high yield because they're currently booming and, therefore, have a lot of earnings they can share with investors, or they're going through a cyclical downturn and, therefore, their yield is elevated but on precarious footing.

A recent example of this includes LyondellBasell Industries (LYB), which ended up slashing its dividend because it went through a period of economic weakness that forced it to cut its dividend. Even before then, LYB paid out a hefty special dividend to shareholders that temporarily boosted its yield, but if you had bought in the wake of that special dividend, you would have seen your stock price plummet by well over 50%. Instead, you want a business model that generates stable cash flows across cycles, therefore leading to much more dependable dividend income over time.

Lesson 4: Dividend Growth Is The Engine Of Long-Term Wealth

The fourth lesson is that dividend growth is a key component to compounding wealth over time. Sure, high current yield is great in terms of accelerating your path to early retirement, and you can reinvest retained dividends to create your own dividend growth. However, companies that consistently grow their own dividends do more than just increase the passive income you receive each year. This signals a sustained and potentially strengthening underlying business model and competitive positioning, as well as profitability, and also signals ongoing balance sheet prudence and optimism about the future. After all, it is much easier to sustain a dividend than to grow it if the company is facing challenges and concerns. Thus, a consistently growing dividend is one of the surest signs out there that the stock you hold is increasing in intrinsic value over time.

Lesson 5: The Capital Recycling Secret Most Dividend Investors Never Discover

A fifth important lesson that I learned is that capital recycling often beats passive buying and holding when you're dealing with high-yield stocks. The underlying principle here is that high-yielding stocks are generally not the wonderful businesses that Warren Buffett advocates for buying and holding forever.

The reason for this is that when a company is a truly wonderful long-term compounder, that means it generally is able to generate very high returns on invested capital, which incentivizes it to retain its capital rather than return it to shareholders. Thus, if a company lacks these opportunities, it often sees that returning the capital to shareholders instead is a better approach.

The downside, of course, is that these are not great buy-and-hold-forever compounders; however, the positive is that they are much easier to value since they are not significant growers and instead return almost all of their profits to shareholders as dividends. Therefore, investors can have a much better sense of what a fair price is for these stocks.

While this certainly can help protect against downside risk by significantly overpaying for stock, the other benefit that many miss out on is that it opens up the ability to recycle capital regularly on an accretive basis. You can feel fairly certain you're selling a stock at or near fair value, and then you can recycle the capital into stocks you have a pretty strong sense are undervalued, especially if you purchase a diversified basket of them. You can then rinse and repeat this process over and over and over again to generate not only attractive returns but actually accelerate the returns that you would otherwise have gotten by simply buying and holding a high-yield stock. Not only can you accelerate the compounding of your total returns, but you can also accelerate the compounding of your income growth if you sell one stock and recycle into an opportunity that has a higher yield.

Why Getting These Five Rules Right Changes Everything

While dividend investing looks easy on the surface, as these five rules point out, there's actually a lot more to it than meets the eye. These include:

  • Simply be cautious when investing in higher-yielding stocks and make sure you're not missing the story or an important risk factor.

  • Taking a deeper dive than simply surface-level metrics when evaluating the sustainability of a dividend.

  • Ensuring that the business model itself is a durable and defensive one in nature.

  • Ensuring that you are investing in an adequate number of dividend growers rather than just high yielders that have a stagnant dividend that they're fighting to maintain.

  • Implementing a capital recycling strategy, especially in a tax-sheltered account.

Many nuances to dividend investing can be the difference between someone who blows up their portfolio by chasing yield traps, someone who achieves decent but unremarkable total returns over time, and those who generate significant total return outperformance. By implementing these principles and several others, I've been able to significantly outperform the S&P 500 and broader dividend sector (SPYD)(VIG) with below-market beta in my 7-8% yielding portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Salesforce: Billions Of Reasons To Own
data.description:
Salesforce, Inc. (CRM) ripped higher as the market appreciated the large share buyback, but the SaaSpocalypse fears have returned. The leading CRM solution continues reporting strong growth with impressive cash flows while the stock trades at multi-year lows. My investment thesis is bullish on the opportunity to dip-buy a leading software play alongside a massive share buyback.

Massive Stock Buyback

Salesforce announced major plans to repurchase $50 billion worth of shares along with the FQ4'26 results. The stock only has a market cap of around $170 billion now, so the buyback is significant to the valuation.

Back on March 11, Salesforce announced a $25 billion accelerated share repurchase, ASR, agreement. The company completed the prepayment of the $25 billion via a debt offering, and Salesforce already took initial delivery of 103 million shares, ~80% of the shares to be repurchased under this ASR and fully settled by FQ3'27.

The CFO made the following statement about the share repurchase:

This $25 billion ASR reflects our increased conviction in the durability of our growth and cash flow trajectory.

The market doesn't like that the company went to market to borrow $25 billion in senior notes to cover the up-front ASR. Salesforce had the option to repay debt over time or repurchase shares over time, and the company saw the current price as too attractive to pass up.

Salesforce ended FY26 with a cash balance of $9.6 billion and $14.4 billion in debt. The company had a strong balance sheet entering the share repurchase program.

Salesforce generated $14.4 billion in free cash flow last year. The forecast is for up to 10% cash flow growth in FY27 for a free cash flow target in the $16 billion range. In essence, the software company has the cash on hand and cash flow in FY27 to cover the massive ASR.

The company already had a strong net payout yield entering the ASR at 7.8%, but this $25 billion buyback took the metric to the next level. The NPY measures the combined dividend yield and net share buyback yields versus the market cap, with Salesforce already having a strong buyback combined with a minimal 1% dividend yield.

The new NPY will shoot to 15% based on this ASR alone and much higher depending on how Salesforce handles the additional $25 billion share buyback approval. Considering the balance sheet will suddenly have nearly $40 billion in debt, the market clearly prefers a slow process to additional buybacks, with most free cash flow going to repaying debt, not share buybacks for now.

For now, the NPY will hit ~21% due to Salesforce repurchasing a net $9.5 billion worth of shares in the prior 3 quarters. When combined with the $25 billion ASR, the company will have a TTM share buyback of $34.5 billion, or ~20%, while the dividend yield adds another 1% to the yield.

SaaSpocalypse

Salesforce continues to report strong growth for a company with nearly $40 billion in software sales. The company reported FQ4 sales grew 12.1% and guided to FY27 growth topping 10% and reaching towards 11%.

The stock trades at only 14x the FY27 consensus analyst EPS targets of $13.20, which doesn't appear to even factor in a reduction in the share count topping 10% already. Hence, the attractiveness of management loading up on share buybacks while the stock is cheap, trading at up to half of the forward P/E multiple of the last couple of years.

The biggest risk to the story is AI eating software businesses. The big pushback is that enterprises aren't going to trust their customer relations systems used by sales forces to a Vibe coding process that doesn't provide a complete enterprise level of security and support.

New AI startups could eat into the business, so investors clearly want Salesforce to quickly cut the debt level to reduce any balance sheet risk and the interest expense from a high debt level. Ultimately, the company will lack the financial flexibility to address any future opportunities unless debt is quickly repaid.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Salesforce is retesting the recent lows around $180. Investors should use any successful test as an opportunity to start loading up on the leading CRM business, with AI fears overplayed and the company aggressively buying cheap shares to boost earnings. I rate CRM a "Strong Buy."

data.title: Pay To Play In The Strait Of Hormuz
data.description:
The fate of the Strait of Hormuz is the most important consideration for much of the world right now, given how much oil transits through it—1 in 5 barrels globally. Since the outbreak of the U.S.-Iran War just under a month ago, transit has been perilous, with civilian vessels having been hit by the Iranian military.

Now that oil is really starting to become thin around certain parts of the world, like Japan and Asia at large, Tehran has put out a new policy: transit is possible for non-American and Israeli ships, but it comes with a catch: you have to pay the toll, and the toll is $2M, preferably in yuan or crypto.

What at first appeared to me as a rogue IRGC commander trying to profit from the situation is now being considered by the Iranian parliament, which may codify a toll for ships passing through Hormuz.

Hormuz Isn't Like The Suez Or Panama Canals

One of the reasons why countries can take fees on transit through tight corridors (which was the justification for Iran imposing fees) is because the vessels have to actually enter the country to transit. But the Strait of Hormuz doesn't exist inside any country's physical boundaries, and the part where ships transit actually sits inside Oman's exclusive economic zone.

Even then, UNCLOS, international maritime law, holds that straits cannot be monetized like canals. Coastal nations are unable to do what Iran is trying to do, at least legally.

But international maritime law obviously does not matter here. What matters is who will pay the illegal toll. That figure is still lost in the fog of war, and it's unclear how many ships, if any at all, have actually paid Iran.

One sign that we can use to confirm this is happening is that ships that have passed through the strait—before these new 10 just announced, as those were a “present to Trump,” as said by the IRGC—have been rerouting themselves close to the Iranian coastline. This is presumably so that the IRGC, whose projection off the coastline is limited and who reportedly do not have much apart from satellite communications tools, no radar, etc., can verify the identity of the ship before allowing passage.

How long this will go on for is unclear, but this exact route has been taken by several tankers now.

The U.S. Could End This Soon

It's doubtful from my view that the U.S. and its Gulf allies will allow this kind of extortion, especially since it will be directly impacting the major exporters in the region like Saudi Arabia and the UAE.

For any toll to be respected, there must be enough force to back it up. It's unclear if Iran could actually enforce this, or for how long, especially if the U.S. military came to interdict in the islands in the strait. I expect that is the plan with the Marine expeditionary unit and paratroopers recently routed to the Middle East, not Kharg Island. For reference, I'm talking about the two little islands between which the Pakistani oil tanker made its route in the above image.

Holding Hormuz (north) and Larak islands would be a much easier feat than holding onto Kharg, much farther into the strait. They are largely uninhabited compared to the other islands and give the U.S. a highly strategic outpost to ensure safe passage of ships through the normal shipping lanes and intercept any Iranian attacks.

The Economic Implications Of The Toll

The only future in which I see this toll becoming a more permanent fixture of the strait is if it is agreed to as part of a peace deal in exchange for other concessions. However, I find that incredibly doubtful.

For now, the economic implications are minimal, but if this toll becomes long-term, consider that the economic ramifications could snowball and get unruly quickly. The $2M amount is just too high for anything but ransom negotiations.

Some perspective is needed here on what $2M actually means for a tanker passing through the strait. Consider that a VLCC, the largest and most common tanker moving oil out of the strait, transports ~2M barrels. At the current crisis pricing for oil, $100, the toll is 1% of cargo value. But at the price oil was at a month ago, $67, this represents a far greater share.

Tanker operations are unlikely to be keen on paying this, as it would eat into their margins at ludicrous rates. Consider that Suez's highest rate is $250,000/transit, and Panama's is $300,000/transit. Though smaller ships with less cargo value transit here, the difference is stark.

Major shipping firms that operate VLCCs that transit the strait frequently, like Frontline Plc (FRO), do not have enough cash on hand or the ability to sustainably raise the cash to cover this kind of toll for their fleet.

They operate 42 VLCCs and could not cover their tolls for a given year with their cash-on-hand, assuming all other expenses stay the same. It would bankrupt them, and they are one of the stronger shipping firms. Smaller firms will go under quickly if this is the new norm (another reason I doubt this toll will become permanent).

There is a question about the near term for prices if the toll is getting paid to stave off shortages in Asian economies. I would expect the oil coming out of the Gulf currently to carry a premium even after escalation.

This means that the shock will be felt unevenly, and regionality will be on display; Asian economies like Japan, Korea, and Taiwan will likely face the worst of it, as they buy oil and natural gas from one of the most expensive sources currently. The UAE and Qatar are major exporters to the region, and both have crude fields sitting at over $150/barrel. These countries have major exposure to these prices; for example, a Japanese firm, INPEX (IPXHY), has a 12% stake in the Upper Zakum field.

Note: You can find these prices here.

Contrast that to U.S.-born crude, which sells for far less. There are blends in the U.S. going for as low as $74/barrel. Firms in the U.S. are not paying nearly the same as Asian firms for oil currently. Similar prices apply for Mexican and South American crude, and so the Western Hemisphere is operating on a premium, but nothing like the East is.

I would expect this divergence to continue for the remainder of the conflict but to stay in place after if the Iranian toll is stickier than I think it will be. That would structurally change the market and could essentially codify a premium for Gulf oil over Western oil.

That could lead to inflation for nations like Japan, Korea, and Taiwan as energy prices stay elevated in the long term and would likely bring even more demand for U.S. crude in the future. Again, I don't see that as likely, since I don't believe a toll will be allowed to remain in the strait.

One thing I will say is to be careful of speculating on derivatives of this trade, like oil freight rates. These have acted like a call option on conflict in the Middle East and are a benefit for shipping firms like FRO in the short term but damaging for the economy in the long term. Investors are speculating on these rates through funds like the BWET ETF. All I can say is that I think that ship has sailed—pun intended—and it's likely too late to jump into the trade. I would believe that we're at or near a peak for freight rates.

Conclusion

The Iranians are attempting to toll the ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz, and rumors are that some ships are paying it. The Iranian government is attempting to implement the toll officially. Despite its international illegality, the reality is that this toll could become a part of some broader quid pro quo to establish peace and safe transit, though I find that doubtful.

If a toll remains in force for any long period of time, even if it is another month, it will weigh heavily on shipping firms. I expect it to exaggerate the current premium experienced by Gulf oil, and a long-term agreement of payment for transit would meaningfully decouple Eastern and Western oil prices on a more permanent basis. If that happens, I expect Eastern inflation to jump far faster than Western inflation when it comes to global oil prices and how they are affecting economies through higher gas and diesel costs, etc.

The fog of war is still very thick, and it's unclear how long negotiations will go on for, if the U.S. will deploy troops to take strategic outposts to ensure passage and break the toll, or what the fate of the war itself will be.

I hope for peace.

Thanks for reading.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Buy The Dip: Best Stocks To Buy As Stagflation Fear Overshadows Growth Potential
data.description:
Stagflation Fears Present Contrarian Opportunities

Fears of stagflation have returned to financial media headlines as oil prices surge, and the one-time price distortions of tariffs are appearing in inflation reports. This backdrop has prompted investors to rotate away from certain sectors, such as Basic Materials (XLB), Consumer Discretionary (XLY), and Information Technology (XLK). However, today’s backdrop differs significantly from the 1970s. As Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell noted at last week’s FOMC press meeting, "When we use the term stagflation, I always have to point out, that was a 1970s term at a time when unemployment was in double figures, and inflation was really high, and the Misery Index was super high… And that's not the case right now."

'Misery Index' Still Less Than Half the 70s and 80s Peaks

A hint of how certain sectors that are either beaten down or held back from higher price appreciation appeared this week when headlines suggested war with Iran may be winding down. Basic Materials stocks, particularly miners, as well as Consumer Discretionary and certain Information Technology names, jumped higher on the news. For investors willing to lean contrarian while fear still pervades market sentiment, now can be an opportune time to buy high-quality, Quant Strong Buys that can rebound quickly as sentiment normalizes.

How I Selected the Best Stocks to Buy the Stagflation Fear

To select the five best stocks recently held back by stagflation fear, I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener and targeted Quant Strong Buys. Then I selected the Sector & Industry filter so I could sort for targeted sectors, including Basic Materials, Consumer Discretionary, and Information Technology.

1. Coeur Mining, Inc. (CDE)

  • Market Capitalization: $18.46B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 9 out of 282

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 3 out of 49

  • Sector: Materials

  • Industry: Gold

As markets have plummeted, I recently wrote two articles to capture potential buy-the-dip opportunities:

  • Buy the Dip: Best Stocks With Forward EPS Growth Above 40%

  • Buy the Dip: Top Tech Stocks After The Pullback

Starting with what may be one of the best “buy the dip” opportunities in the market now, Coeur Mining has been caught in the crosscurrents of a stronger dollar and weaker market sentiment, which have beaten down miners, despite strong fundamentals. CDE has fallen more than 25% over the past month, but it bounced 5% higher this week through midday Wednesday, as Iran tensions and oil prices eased. The Magnificent Seven stocks, or "Lag 7," have fallen substantially behind the remaining 493 stocks in the S&P 500. As oil prices and inflation fears fade further, downward pressure on gold and silver prices can also lift, making miners with a strong combination of valuation and growth metrics, such as CDE, early beneficiaries.

CDE’s forward Non-GAAP P/E of 7.49 gives it a 52% discount to sector peers, and on a trailing basis, its 0.04 PEG suggests nearly an 84% discount after factoring in its growth. For some perspective, Coeur's Q4 earnings results reflected a period when the price of gold ranged from about $3850 to $4500 per troy ounce. Gold’s price, at the time of this writing, was about $4550, which suggests miners still enjoy similar support in Q1 2026. Shifting to Coeur’s forward growth, standout metrics are highlighted by forward revenue growth of 83.71%, which is over 15x the sector median. Underscoring the company’s operational efficiency, its forward EBITDA growth of over 138% and operating cash flow of 171% crush sector peers by similar margins.

With fears of stagnation likely overdone, CDE offers upside potential as investors rotate back into cyclicals and aggressive-growth areas, which sets the stage for my next rebound candidate.

2. Sandisk Corporation (SNDK)

  • Market Capitalization: $100.05B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 1 out of 524

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 1 out of 26

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Technology Hardware, Storage, and Peripherals

One of the holdings in my Pro Quant Portfolio, Sandisk, is a classic case of an AI growth story overshadowed by stagflationary fears. As a key player in flash storage and memory, the company is tied to data center growth and related infrastructure buildouts, which are trends that remain intact regardless of short-term inflation concerns. The recent weakness in tech reflects broad-based selling in reaction to higher Treasury yields and dollar strength, not a deterioration in fundamentals, which are rich for SanDisk, one of the most-attractive value+growth stories in the market today.

While many top-rated AI stocks have strong growth metrics, their valuations are high relative to the sector. However, SanDisk’s valuation metrics reveal attractive discounts. For example, it’s rare to see a stock that has appreciated over 1,100% in one year like Sandisk, but still has a forward Non-GAAP forward P/E of 16.83, offering nearly a 22% discount to the sector median. Even more impressive is the forward PEG 0.08, which represents a 94% discount to peers. The company’s PEG is so low because of its forward earnings growth, which is driven by extreme AI-related demand for high-performance storage. Only a few companies, which include Micron Technology (MU), one of Alpha Picks’ top holdings, are in such a fortunate position.

Consecutively topping earnings, SNDK reported a Q2 2026 EPS of $6.20, a $2.66 beat and revenue of $3.03 billion, up a massive 61.25% year-over-year. Sandisk is positioned as a primary beneficiary of an AI-fueled NAND flash supercycle, with gross margins exceeding 50% and expectations of further improvement. As investors return their focus to cyclical growth rather than macro noise, SNDK is well positioned to continue its upward trajectory, leading to another technology name with similar AI leverage.

3. Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE)

  • Market Capitalization: $57.26B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 3 out of 524

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 1 out of 37

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Communications Equipment

A standout in both my Alpha Picks and Pro Quant Portfolio stocks, Lumentum has also experienced downside pressure in recent months. However, its past momentum is showing signs of return as its exposure to optical networking and AI-related infrastructure growth is impossible for bullish investors to ignore. Demand for high-speed connectivity and data transmission continues to surge, driven by hyperscaler investment, which has yet to show sensitivity to short-term macro pressures. Recent earnings strength and attractive key valuation metrics prove its Quant Strong Buy worthiness.

LITE offers a case in point on why the PEG ratio matters more in a growth environment than the superficial P/E that many investors focus on. The company's 101.07 Non-GAAP forward P/E ratio appears as a red flag on the surface, explaining the 'F' for this metric in its Valuation Factor Grade scorecard. But a quick glance lower reveals the 0.77 PEG, which indicates more than a 40% discount to the sector median. This suggests that a continuation of growth leaves room for more price appreciation, especially as macro headline noise fades.

LITE’s forward EBITDA growth of nearly 133% is more than 8x the sector median’s 15.24%, while its forward EPS Long Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) supports. LITE has also strengthened its position as a strategic partner in providing critical laser supply following NVIDIA Corporation’s (NVDA) $2 billion investment in the company, and was recently announced as a new addition to the S&P 500 (SP 500), effective March 23. A recent announcement revealing a $2B quarterly revenue run rate reinforces its long-term growth potential. As stagflation fears fade and investors rotate back into high-growth technology, LITE stands to benefit, leading to a rebound story, this time tied to an overlooked consumer discretionary idea.

4. American Public Education, Inc. (APEI)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.08B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 2 out of 480

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 1 out of 26

  • Sector: Consumer Discretionary

  • Industry: Education Services

American Public Education has been swept up in market sentiment fears around consumer discretionary spending that can come with stagflation, despite its strong fundamentals in a segment with relatively stable demand. As a provider of online postsecondary education and workforce training, APEI benefits from forward growth potential that runs contrary to expectations during periods of economic uncertainty. This disconnect sets up an opportunity for investors to buy while the market looks the other way. This stock has its most attractive Quant Factors displayed in Growth and Revision grades, both of which show impressive improvement over the past six months.

While APEI’s year-over-year revenue growth of 3.89% scores just a little better than the sector median, its forward EBIT growth of 33.37% gives a better picture of its operating performance, which is more than 5x the sector median. At its recent Q4 quarterly earnings call, management provided an outlook expectation for continued enrollment momentum and margin expansion, outlining $685M-$695M 2026 revenue guidance. This forward momentum supports the company’s A+ Revisions Factor Grade, which is a predictive measure of price appreciation.

With improving fundamentals and increasing demand for its services, APEI is positioned for greater price appreciation as macro fears fade. A shift in sentiment can also benefit our final stock and opportunity tied to recovery strength.

5. DiamondRock Hospitality Company (DRH)

  • Market Capitalization: $1.97B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 8 out of 169

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 03/26/2026): 2 out of 14

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Hotel & Resort REITs

DiamondRock Hospitality has faced downside pressure amid concerns that higher inflation and gas prices could decrease travel demand. However, the upper-half of the so-called "K-economy" consumer spending remains resilient, and demand in leisure and group travel continues to hold up better than expected. The recent pullback is more a reflection of short-term macro-driven caution than a real deterioration in fundamentals. This sets up an opportunity to buy a consumer discretionary-related REIT with a strong valuation/growth setup, as illustrated by Quant Factor Grades.

A more telling ratio for an REIT is the forward P/FFO, which is 8.38, supporting a 35.62% discount to sector peers. This metric further supports its 'B-' valuation grade, indicating investors are paying less for each dollar of recurring cash flow.

DRH’s sector-leading 15.81% AFFO Growth (3Y Hist. CAGR), over 6x the sector median, indicates the REIT has a history of expanding its cash flow much faster than peers. The 81.71% Dividend Growth Rate 3Y (CAGR), which is more than 30x the sector median, demonstrates extreme confidence in future cash flows and a commitment to returning capital. DRH’s yield is a solid 4.24%. When stagflation fears subside and recession expectations ease, travel-related REITs like DRH can benefit as investors rotate back into cyclical areas. This positions DRH as another recovery play and rounds out my list of five stocks primed to benefit from improving sentiment.

Conclusion: Stagflation Fears Create Opportunities and Risk

In combination, these stocks highlight a broader theme in which investors may be overestimating the risk of stagflation while underappreciating the potential for a return to growth and cyclical demand. From AI-driven tech to global education and travel, each name has either been pulled lower or higher returns have been capped by fear that is probably overdone. While I have acknowledged conditions for mild stagflation, the 1970s environment is not likely to repeat. As fears tied to oil prices, tariffs, and associated inflation begin to fade, these dislocations can reverse quickly, as we saw in price jumps this week when hopes of an end to the war in Iran surfaced. That said, the economic and market environment remains fluid and unpredictable on a daily basis, and ripple effects from higher oil prices can persist for months after the conflict ends. In volatile markets, aggressive investors can be selective, while other investors may want to be more cautious.
